confirmed.title: Action-form validation ignores field labels (custom messages()/attributes()) — :attribute renders raw snake_case
confirmed.detail: execute() calls $request->validate($rules) with NO messages and NO attributes array. This is the exact gap round 2 fixed for the main CRUD path in ResourceController.php:763, but it was never carried over to the Action form-submission path. Action form fields carry getLabel() values (HasForm::getFormValidationRules() at packages/actions/src/Concerns/HasForm.php:91 returns only rules, dropping labels), so a failed action-form validation renders ':attribute' as the raw snake_case field name and any field-level custom messages are silently dropped. Build an attributes map from each form field's getLabel() (passed through __()) and pass it as the 3rd/4th arg to validate().
confirmed.why_real:
Verified against source and the round 1-5 ledger.

REAL: ActionController::execute() at packages/actions/src/Http/Controllers/ActionController.php:115 calls `$request->validate($rules)` with NO 2nd/3rd argument. The rules come from HasForm::getFormValidationRules() (packages/actions/src/Concerns/HasForm.php:91-103), which returns ONLY rules keyed by field name and drops both labels and custom messages. Action form fields are Arqel\Fields\Field instances that DO carry: (a) a localized label via Field::getLabel() (Field.php:181, passes through __()/localizeLabel), and (b) per-field custom messages via HasValidation::validationMessage()/getValidationMessages() (packages/fields/src/Concerns/HasValidation.php). Because neither an attributes map nor a messages map is passed to validate(), a failed action-form validation renders `:attribute` as the raw snake_case field key and silently drops any validationMessage() the app declared. This is a genuine user-facing i18n/error-copy defect on the action form-submission path.

PARITY CONFIRMED: This is exactly the gap round 2 closed for the main CRUD path. ResourceController::validated() at lines 763-777 now calls `$request->validate($rules, $this->extractMessages($fields), $this->extractAttributes($fields))`, both derived from label-bearing effectiveFields(). The Action path never received the analogous treatment.

NOT A DUPLICATE: No ledger entry references ActionController or HasForm for the messages/attributes gap. The only round-2 attributes/messages entry is ResourceController.php:763 (CRUD path). The round-1 Action.php:327 entry concerns verbatim serialization of $label/successNotification/failureNotification in toArray() — a distinct issue (display serialization, not validator inputs).

confirmed.title: Select/MultiSelect static & closure option LABELS serialized verbatim — never passed through __()
confirmed.detail: resolveOptions() returns $this->staticOptions (and closure results) as-is; FieldSchemaSerializer.php:213-217 injects them into props.options untouched, and the React SelectInput renders the values verbatim. So options(['draft' => 'Draft', 'published' => 'Published']) ship English no matter the locale. This is the missed sibling of the round-1 Field::$label / Column::$label / Action::$label trans() fixes — option labels are the same kind of human-facing label but were never wired through __(). Same gap repeats in packages/table/src/Columns/SelectColumn.php:117 (toArray 'options' => resolveOptions()) and packages/table/src/Filters/SelectFilter.php. Pass each option label value through __() at resolve/serialize time.
confirmed.why_real: Confirmed and novel. SelectField::resolveOptions() (packages/fields/src/Types/SelectField.php:177) returns $this->staticOptions verbatim and closure results untouched; getTypeSpecificProps() (line 351) injects them into props['options'] with no __()/trans(). The round-1 fix added Field::localizeLabel() (Field.php:181-204) for the field's own $label via trans(), but option labels — the same class of human-facing label — were never routed through it. The React SelectInput.tsx:41 renders {opt.label} verbatim with no t(), so client-side translation does not save it either. The relationship-options path in FieldSchemaSerializer.php:213-216 likewise injects resolved labels untouched. Both named table siblings reproduce: SelectColumn.php:117 ('options' => resolveOptions()) and SelectFilter.php:147 (normalizeOptions over resolveOptions) — neither calls __(). Net effect: options(['draft'=>'Draft','published'=>'Published']) ship English under any locale. Ledger check: rounds 1-5 cover Field::$label, Column::$label, Action::$label, TernaryFilter true/false labels, a single hardcoded JS 'All' option in DashboardFilters, and Widget heading — but no entry for Select/MultiSelect static/closure OPTION labels nor SelectColumn/SelectFilter options. Distinct, uncovered, user-facing surface. Not a dev-only string; not already wired.

confirmed.title: Export absolute date/datetime cells use raw Carbon ->format() — textual month/day tokens (F/M/l/D) render in English
confirmed.detail: The round-3 fix bound only the 'since' mode to the request locale (line 46-48 ->locale()->diffForHumans()). The absolute date/datetime branch returns $value->format($format), which is locale-blind: a column declared with textual tokens (e.g. ->dateTime('d M Y') or ->date('l, d F Y')) emits English month/day names in the export even for a pt_BR/de panel, diverging from the on-screen Intl-formatted cell. Use Carbon::instance($value)->locale($this->activeLocale())->translatedFormat($format) (or isoFormat) so textual tokens localize.
confirmed.why_real:
Verified against /home/diogo/PhpstormProjects/arqel/packages/export/src/Exporters/FormatsDateCells.php and the ledger.

The absolute date/datetime branch at line 53 returns `$value->format(is_string($format) ? $format : 'Y-m-d')` — a raw Carbon/DateTimeInterface ->format() call that is locale-blind. When a DateColumn declares a format string with textual tokens (F/M/l/D, e.g. ->date('d F Y') or ->dateTime('l, d M Y')), ->format() emits English month/day names regardless of the app/request locale. The on-screen DateCell (packages-js/ui/src/table/cells.tsx) uses Intl-based localized formatting (round-2 fixes), so a pt_BR/de panel renders localized month names on screen but English in the export — a real user-facing divergence in exported output. The in-file activeLocale() helper already exists, so the fix (Carbon::instance($value)->locale($this->activeLocale())->translatedFormat($format)) is well-supported.

NOT a duplicate: the only ledger entry for this file is round-3 line 43, which covers exclusively the `since` (relative diffForHumans) branch — visible as the fix at lines 46-48. That is a different branch (line 53 vs 46), different line, and a different defect class (textual-token localization vs relative-string localization). The absolute branch was left unwired.

Confidence is med rather than high because the defect only manifests for columns using textual date tokens; the DateColumn default (Y-m-d, all-numeric) is locale-invariant and unaffected, so the blast radius depends on user-declared formats. Still genuinely reproducible and novel.

confirmed.title: AiImageInput file-size display hardcodes en-US decimal and English unit labels (MB/KB/B)
confirmed.detail: formatBytes() builds `${(bytes/1_048_576).toFixed(1)} MB` etc. — toFixed always emits a '.' decimal (wrong for comma-decimal locales) and the unit suffixes 'MB'/'KB'/'B' are hardcoded English literals. The output is shown to the user (lines 195-196 feed size/max into a visible validation message). Round 5 localized AiImageInput's 'File too large' string but left the size value formatting English. Use Intl.NumberFormat(locale, {maximumFractionDigits:1}) and translate/parameterize the unit, or Intl.NumberFormat with style:'unit', unit:'megabyte'.
confirmed.why_real:
Confirmed in source. packages-js/ai/src/AiImageInput.tsx:114-122 defines formatBytes() which builds size strings with `.toFixed(1)` (always a '.' decimal, wrong for comma-decimal locales like pt-BR/de-DE) and hardcoded English unit literals 'MB'/'KB'/'B'. This output is user-facing: at lines 194-197 it is interpolated as the :size and :max params of the otherwise-localized arqel.ai.file_too_large message, which renders in the destructive Alert (lines 426-430). So even with a translated message, the user sees an English-formatted number and English unit (e.g. 'Arquivo muito grande: 5.2 MB (max 2.0 MB)').

Not dev-only: it reaches the validation banner shown to the user. Not already wired: formatBytes uses no Intl/locale awareness. Correct fix is Intl.NumberFormat(locale, {maximumFractionDigits:1}) plus a localized/Intl unit (style:'unit', unit:'megabyte').

Ledger check: the only AiImageInput entry is Round 5 line 193 'File too large validation error hardcoded English', which covers wiring the message STRING through t(). It does not cover the formatBytes() number/unit formatting at line 114. This is genuinely the next layer left unfixed by Round 5, so novel, not a duplicate. Confidence med rather than high because file-size units are sometimes intentionally left untranslated in real UIs, but the toFixed decimal-separator divergence inside a translated message is a legitimate locale-format defect.

confirmed.title: VersionDiff empty-state text 'No changes to display.' hardcoded English
confirmed.detail:
The empty comparison view renders a hardcoded English

No changes to display.

with no translation hook. VersionTimeline.tsx was swept in rounds 2/4 but VersionDiff.tsx has no useArqelTranslations usage at all. App-keyed: should pull from arqel.versioning.* (e.g. arqel.versioning.no_changes).
confirmed.why_real:
Verified at packages-js/versioning/src/VersionDiff.tsx:206: the empty-state branch renders a literal

No changes to display.

inside a CardContent. The component imports only from '@arqel-dev/ui' and 'react' — there is no useArqelTranslations/t() usage anywhere in the file, so this string cannot be localized. It is genuinely user-facing visible body text (not a dev-only or purely-aria string), shown whenever the before/after comparison has no visible diff entries. Novel vs ledger: the i18n-fixed-ledger.json contains VersionTimeline.tsx entries (rounds 2 and 4) but NO VersionDiff.tsx entry in any of rounds 1-5. The finding explicitly and correctly distinguishes the two sibling files. Line number and content match exactly. App-keyed suggestion (arqel.versioning.*) is consistent with the existing convention. Confirmed real and not a duplicate.

confirmed.title: VersionDiff 'Modified' badge label hardcoded
confirmed.detail: Long-text block diff renders <Badge variant="default">Modified</Badge> as literal English. Framework-hardcoded; no t().
confirmed.why_real:
Confirmed at /home/diogo/PhpstormProjects/arqel/packages-js/versioning/src/VersionDiff.tsx:179: `<Badge variant="default">Modified</Badge>` renders the literal English word "Modified" for the long-text block-diff case. This is a user-facing framework UI component (the version-diff comparator rendered to end users), not a dev-only/debug string. The file imports only `Badge, Card, CardContent` from @arqel-dev/ui and `JSX, useMemo` from react — there is NO translation hook (useArqelTranslations/t/__) anywhere in scope, so the label cannot be localized. The grep "t(" hits are false positives (Array.from / split). Reproduces genuinely.

Not a duplicate: I scanned the entire rounds 1-5 ledger. All versioning-package entries reference VersionTimeline.tsx (line 36 round 2; lines 124 and 179 round 4). VersionDiff.tsx appears NOWHERE in the ledger. Note that ledger round-4 entry "VersionTimeline.tsx line 179" is a different file (VersionTimeline, not VersionDiff) despite the coincidentally identical line number, so it does not cover this finding. Novel and real.

(Aside: line 206 "No changes to display." is a sibling hardcoded string in the same file, also unledgered, but the finding is scoped to line 179 which stands on its own.)

confirmed.title: VersionDiff card aria-label 'Field comparison' hardcoded (two occurrences)
confirmed.detail: Both the empty-state Card (L204) and the populated Card (L213) use aria-label="Field comparison" as literal English. Screen-reader landmark name never localized.
confirmed.why_real: Reproduces: packages-js/versioning/src/VersionDiff.tsx L204 (empty-state Card) and L213 (populated Card) both use aria-label="Field comparison" as a literal English string. aria-label is the accessible name for the Card landmark, so this is genuinely surfaced to screen-reader users and never localized — a real user-facing a11y/i18n defect. The component imports/uses no translation hook (no useArqelTranslations/t()/__() — grep matches were unrelated to translation), confirming the file was never i18n-wired (L206 "No changes to display." is also hardcoded). Ledger check: zero VersionDiff entries across rounds 1-5; the closely related work touched VersionTimeline.tsx (rounds 2 and 4), not VersionDiff.tsx, so this is novel, not a duplicate.

confirmed.title: VersionDiff value-cell aria-label 'no previous value'/'no new value' hardcoded
confirmed.detail: renderValueCell sets aria-label={side === 'before' ? 'no previous value' : 'no new value'} for the em-dash placeholder on added/removed fields. Hardcoded English a11y text.
confirmed.why_real: Verified at packages-js/versioning/src/VersionDiff.tsx:124: renderValueCell sets `aria-label={side === 'before' ? 'no previous value' : 'no new value'}` on the em-dash placeholder span for added/removed fields. This is a genuinely user-facing accessibility string (announced by screen readers), in a shipped production component (not test/dev/Storybook), and is hardcoded English. Novelty: the ledger has zero VersionDiff entries (grep -c = 0). All versioning-package ledger entries target VersionTimeline.tsx (rounds 2 and 4); even the round-4 entry at 'line 124' is in VersionTimeline.tsx with different strings ('Loading versions'/'Version history'), a different file. Therefore real and not a duplicate. Confidence high.

(data-testid="state-transition-empty-state"). confirmed.title: StateTransition empty-state 'No transitions available.' hardcoded English
confirmed.detail: When visibleTransitions is empty the component renders literal English 'No transitions available.'; no translation hook.
confirmed.why_real: Verified at /home/diogo/PhpstormProjects/arqel/packages-js/workflow/src/StateTransition.tsx:184. When visibleTransitions.length === 0 the component renders a literal English string "No transitions available." inside a user-visible <span data-testid="state-transition-empty-transitions">. (line 163) shares the same defect class. This is a real, user-facing (not dev-only, not aria-only) string with no translation wiring. confirmed.title: Audit RecordActivityController 403 'Forbidden' JSON message hardcoded
confirmed.detail: Authorization-denial JSON body returned to the React audit UI uses literal 'Forbidden': new JsonResponse(['message' => 'Forbidden'], 403). Round 5 swept GlobalActivityLogController query-validation messages but not this controller. Should use __('arqel::audit.forbidden').
confirmed.why_real: Reproduces. packages/audit/src/Http/Controllers/RecordActivityController.php:60 returns `new JsonResponse(['message' => 'Forbidden'], 403)` — a hardcoded English string in a response body, never passed through __(). The whole controller uses zero __() calls; its 400 message at line 53 is likewise hardcoded. This is the per-record audit timeline endpoint (AUDIT-002), whose JSON `message` is consumed by the audit UI — the same class of defect the round-5 ledger entry confirmed for the SIBLING GlobalActivityLogController.php:93 query-validation messages (which were fixed to use __('arqel-audit::messages.*')). RecordActivityController is the one controller in this endpoint family that was NOT swept. Novel: it does not appear anywhere in i18n-fixed-ledger.json (the only audit entry is GlobalActivityLogController:93, round 5). Not a dev-only string, not already wired. Caveats: (1) the finding's suggested key `arqel::audit.forbidden` uses the wrong namespace — the real one is `arqel-audit::messages.*`, and no `forbidden` key exists yet, so the fix must add it; (2) the report under-scopes the defect by naming only the 403 — line 53's 400 message is the identical bug in the same file. Neither caveat invalidates the reported defect. Confidence med because user-facingness of a 403 authz-denial body is slightly weaker than a query-validation message, but the round-5 precedent for the sibling controller establishes the framework treats these JSON `message` bodies as localizable.

confirmed.title: Audit RecordActivityController 400 invalid-subject message hardcoded English
confirmed.detail: Validation error JSON shown to API consumers: 'message' => 'subjectType must be a fully-qualified Eloquent model class or a registered morph alias.' — literal English, not passed through __().
confirmed.why_real: Confirmed at packages/audit/src/Http/Controllers/RecordActivityController.php:53. The show() method returns a 400 JsonResponse with 'message' => 'subjectType must be a fully-qualified Eloquent model class or a registered morph alias.' — a literal English string not wrapped in __()/trans(). This branch is reachable (when resolveModelClass() returns null for an invalid subjectType), so it genuinely reproduces and is shown to API consumers (user-facing JSON, not a dev-only/log string). Novel vs the ledger: the round-1-5 ledger has no entry for RecordActivityController.php. The closest entry (round 5) targets the DIFFERENT controller GlobalActivityLogController.php:93 ('hardcoded English query-validation JSON messages'), establishing that this project already treats hardcoded English validation JSON in audit controllers as a real i18n defect — which corroborates rather than duplicates this finding. Not a false positive: no t()/__() is wired here, and the codebase otherwise routes user-facing strings through translation.

confirmed.title: Audit GlobalActivityLogController 403 'Forbidden' HttpException message hardcoded
confirmed.detail: throw new HttpException(403, 'Forbidden') — the denial reason phrase is literal English. Round 5 fixed the query-validation JSON messages in this file but left this authorization-denial message. Should use __().
confirmed.why_real: Confirmed at packages/audit/src/Http/Controllers/GlobalActivityLogController.php:77: `throw new HttpException(403, 'Forbidden')` uses a literal English denial message, not `__()`. The surrounding code (line 90-94) correctly localizes query-validation JSON messages via `__('arqel-audit::messages.invalid_event')`, and lang files (en + pt_BR messages.php) exist with invalid_event/invalid_date keys but NO 'forbidden' key — so this authz-denial string is unlocalized and inconsistent with the file's own convention. Ledger check: the only entry for this file is round 5 line 93, explicitly scoped to 'query-validation JSON messages' — distinct from the line-77 authorization-denial HttpException. Novel, not a duplicate. is_real=true: it is a genuine hardcoded user-facing-ish English string (HttpException message surfaces in the error response). Confidence is med rather than high because Laravel's default exception handler often masks bare HttpException messages with a generic 403 response, so reproduction of the literal 'Forbidden' reaching the end user depends on app config; nonetheless the i18n defect (hardcoded English where __() should be used) is concrete and inconsistent with adjacent localized code.

confirmed.title: Vendored shadcn dialog Close sr-only text hardcoded English
confirmed.detail: Close on the dialog close button is hardcoded English a11y text. Same class of vendored-shadcn a11y gap already logged in round 4 for sidebar.tsx ('Toggle Sidebar'); dialog/sheet were not covered. Used by every Arqel modal built on the vendored Dialog.
confirmed.why_real: Verified at /home/diogo/PhpstormProjects/arqel/packages-js/ui/src/shadcn/ui/dialog.tsx:74: `Close` is hardcoded English screen-reader text on the dialog Close button, with no t()/__() wiring. It is user-facing a11y text announced by screen readers, shipped via the vendored Dialog that backs every Arqel modal (DialogContent showCloseButton path). The i18n-fixed-ledger.json (rounds 1-5) contains only one vendored-shadcn entry: shadcn/ui/sidebar.tsx (round 4, "Toggle Sidebar"). No ledger entry targets shadcn/ui/dialog.tsx or its "Close" sr-only string; the "Dialog.tsx" substring matches in the ledger are ConfirmDialog/ActionFormModal in src/action, not the vendored shadcn primitive. Same defect class as the round-4 sidebar finding but a genuinely distinct, uncovered file. Not dev-only, not already-wired. Real and novel.

confirmed.title: Vendored shadcn sheet Close sr-only text hardcoded English
confirmed.detail: Close on the Sheet/drawer close button, hardcoded English. Drawers (e.g. mobile action bottom-sheet) inherit this unlocalized close affordance.
confirmed.why_real: Confirmed at /home/diogo/PhpstormProjects/arqel/packages-js/ui/src/shadcn/ui/sheet.tsx:78: Close is hardcoded English with no t()/__() wiring. It is gated by showCloseButton (sheet.tsx:49) which defaults to true. The mobile ActionMenu bottom-sheet (ActionMenu.tsx:158 SheetContent) and the mobile Sidebar (sidebar.tsx:186) both consume SheetContent without passing showCloseButton={false}, so this close button and its sr-only label render in real user-facing drawers. It is a screen-reader-exposed string, not dev-only. Not a duplicate: the ledger has no entry for shadcn/ui/sheet.tsx. The nearest entries are sidebar.tsx:277 (a different vendored file, round 4) and the ActionMenu SheetTitle strings (round 1, which are consumer-passed titles, not the Sheet component's internal Close button). This specific defect is novel relative to rounds 1-5.

confirmed.title: useTranslator() public hook ignores pluralization (count) and locale — naive output through the documented React translator API
confirmed.detail: `useTranslator(dict)` is exported as public API from @arqel-dev/react/utils (utils/index.ts line 12) and documented as the React mirror of Laravel __(). But its callback only passes `{ replacements }` to translate() — it never extracts `count` from replacements nor threads a `locale`. So `selectPluralForm` never fires and `Intl.PluralRules` defaults to 'en'. Repro: const t = useTranslator(dict); t('table.selected', { count: 2 }) where the value is '{one} :count item|{other} :count items' renders the FULL pipe-string with both forms (no plural selection) instead of '2 items'. The sibling hooks useArqelTranslations (useArqelTranslations.ts:50) and buildTranslator (i18n/translate.ts:23) both correctly derive count+locale; useTranslator is the lone divergent path and any consumer using it gets broken pluralization in every locale.
confirmed.why_real: Verified by reading packages-js/react/src/utils/translate.ts. useTranslator (line 145) is a public, exported API (utils/index.ts exports it; dist/index.d.ts re-exports from package root) documented in its JSDoc as the dictionary-bound t() mirror of Laravel __(). Its callback (lines 149-151) types the 2nd arg as plain `replacements` and passes only `{ replacements }` to translate() — it never extracts `count` from replacements nor threads a `locale`. translate() (lines 134-135) only calls selectPluralForm when options.count !== undefined, so via useTranslator the pipe-string is never split and the full '{one} :count item|{other} :count items' renders verbatim; Intl.PluralRules also defaults to 'en'. The divergence is real and confirmed: the sibling useArqelTranslations (line 50) correctly does `const count = typeof replacements?.['count']==='number' ? ... ` and threads `{ replacements, locale, count }`. NOT a duplicate: the ledger has no entry for packages-js/react/src/utils/translate.ts or for useTranslator; nearest entries are useArqelTranslations.ts:27 (memoization, different file/defect), TableToolbar.tsx:43 (a consumer-side hardcoded plural), and I18nProvider.tsx:45 (different component) — none cover this hook. Genuine user-facing API defect on documented public surface. Caveat lowering confidence: grep shows no internal framework consumers (all UI uses the correct useArqelTranslations), so real-world breakage is contingent on an external app choosing this exported hook by name — but it is documented public API advertising count/locale via its adjacent TranslateOptions type, so it qualifies.

confirmed.title: Accept-Language parser does not append the language base as a fallback candidate (pt-BR header never matches a 'pt'-only allowlist)
confirmed.detail: parseAcceptLanguage() emits only the normalized full tag per header segment (e.g. 'pt-BR;q=0.9' → ['pt_BR']); it never derives and appends the base 'pt'. Repro: browser sends Accept-Language: pt-BR, panel allowlist is ['en','pt'] (generic Portuguese only). matchAvailable('pt_BR', ['en','pt']) fails (no underscore/base reduction), so resolveLocale falls through to the English default even though a perfectly good Portuguese dir exists. Should expand each candidate to [pt_BR, pt] before allowlist matching.
confirmed.why_real:
Confirmed at code level and not in the round 1-5 ledger.

CODE EVIDENCE:
- packages/core/src/Http/Middleware/SetLocaleMiddleware.php:80 parseAcceptLanguage() splits the header per segment, strips q-values, normalizes '-'→'_', and emits ONLY the full tag (e.g. 'pt-BR;q=0.9' → ['pt_BR']). It never derives the base subtag 'pt'.
- packages/core/src/I18n/TranslationLoader.php:164 matchAvailable() compares ONLY normalized full strings (normalize(entry) === normalize(candidate)). No base-language truncation.

REPRO: A panel that ships generic lang/pt/ and configures arqel.i18n.locales=['en','pt'] (allowlist=['en','pt']) + a browser sending exactly 'Accept-Language: pt-BR' yields candidates ['pt_BR']; matchAvailable('pt_BR', ['en','pt']) returns null, so resolveLocale() falls through to defaultLocale() (English) even though a perfectly serviceable lang/pt/ exists. User-facing effect: a pt-BR user gets the English UI. This is the RFC 4647 'lookup' progressive-truncation behavior (pt-BR → pt) that the parser omits.

NOVELTY vs LEDGER: The closest ledger entries are the round-1 hyphen/underscore divergence fixes (LocaleController.php:38, HandleArqelInertiaRequests.php:422, TranslationLoader.php:71) — those address '-' vs '_' normalization, now handled by matchAvailable's normalize(). This finding is a DISTINCT concern: base-subtag reduction (region tag → base language), not separator normalization. No ledger row touches SetLocaleMiddleware.php:80 or parseAcceptLanguage base-fallback. Round-5 TranslationLoader.php:40 covers per-key dictionary fallback merge, unrelated to header negotiation.

CAVEAT (why not high confidence): The defect is real but narrow. Real-world browsers almost always send the base subtag as an additional lower-q segment ('pt-BR,pt;q=0.9'); in that common case parseAcceptLanguage emits ['pt_BR','pt'] and 'pt' DOES match, masking the bug. It only bites when the header carries a region tag with no accompanying base segment. Still a genuine, fixable user-facing i18n correctness gap (standard practice is to expand each candidate to [pt_BR, pt] before allowlist matching).

confirmed.title: Marketplace root Inertia view hardcodes <html lang="pt-BR"> instead of deriving from app()->getLocale() — SSR/first-paint locale mismatch
confirmed.detail: Every other Arqel layout uses `lang="{{ str_replace('_','-', app()->getLocale()) }}"` (core/resources/views/app.blade.php:2, demo, tenant-demo, showcase). The marketplace app's active Inertia root view (it has @inertia / @inertiaHead) hardcodes lang="pt-BR". Repro: switch the panel to English; the served HTML still declares lang="pt-BR" on first paint. LocaleSwitcher's useEffect later patches document.documentElement.lang client-side, but SSR output, no-JS users, and the first-paint window all expose the wrong language to screen readers and translation tooling.
confirmed.why_real:
CONFIRMED real and novel. Verified at apps/marketplace/resources/views/app.blade.php:14 — `<html lang="pt-BR">` is hardcoded. This IS the app's active Inertia root view: it contains @inertia (line 49) and @inertiaHead (line 46), is named `app` (Inertia's default rootView), and is the only blade view in the app. By contrast, every other Arqel layout derives lang from the runtime locale: packages/core/resources/views/app.blade.php:2 and apps/demo/.../welcome.blade.php:2 both use `lang="{{ str_replace('_', '-', app()->getLocale()) }}"`. So the served first-paint/SSR HTML always declares pt-BR regardless of the actual app locale, which screen readers, no-JS users, and translation tooling consume — a genuine user-facing a11y/i18n defect, not a dev-only string.

Notably the mismatch is real at baseline (not hypothetical): apps/marketplace/config/app.php:83 sets `'locale' => env('APP_LOCALE','en')` and .env.example pins APP_LOCALE=en, so app()->getLocale() is `en` while the markup hardcodes pt-BR — a concrete lang mismatch out of the box.

Caveat (does not change the verdict): the finding's repro narrative is partly inaccurate — there is NO LocaleSwitcher, i18n wiring, or SetLocale middleware anywhere in the marketplace app (grep found none outside vendor/node_modules), so the claimed 'LocaleSwitcher useEffect later patches document.documentElement.lang' does not occur here. But the underlying defect (SSR lang hardcoded rather than derived from app()->getLocale()) genuinely reproduces.

Novelty vs ledger: no ledger entry references apps/marketplace/resources/views/app.blade.php or any hardcoded `<html lang>` in a blade root view. The only `<html lang>` ledger item (round 5, packages-js/i18n/src/LocaleSwitcher.tsx:51) concerns client-side staleness in the i18n package after an Inertia visit — a different file, different layer (JS, not blade SSR), different root cause. Not a duplicate.
